candidate_problems.text: How can the conjecture be operationalized into a predictive structural model that distinguishes when supermajority support will overcome institutional resistance versus when it will fail? The open tension is that the conjecture identifies several load-bearing mechanisms—asymmetric veto points, coalition fragmentation, representational distortion, and incumbent self-protection—but does not specify their relative weights, interaction effects, or threshold conditions. A new problem is to build and test a cross-case framework that predicts reform capacity from institutional structure rather than from opinion levels alone.

What explains variation in successful reform within systems that appear to have the same veto-heavy, incumbent-protecting architecture? The unresolved question is whether the conjecture is missing additional load-bearing variables—such as crisis shocks, elite splits, policy design that reconfigures coalitions, sequencing strategies, or extra-institutional mobilization—that sometimes reorganize majority support into a form the system can absorb. This is worth pursuing because without explaining exceptions, the conjecture risks being descriptively strong but strategically incomplete.

Is there an inherent tradeoff between stability-preserving institutional filters and democratic responsiveness, or can systems be designed to resist rash change without systematically disabling broadly supported reform? The conjecture implies that many institutions marketed as safeguards function as anti-reform structures, but it leaves open the deeper design question of whether those same mechanisms are necessary for other goods such as legitimacy, minority protection, and long-term coordination. This is a significant open problem because it determines whether the remedy is removing friction, redesigning it, or creating alternative reform pathways outside incumbent control.
